edges.description: Reduced tRNA pseudouridylation impairs mitochondrial tRNA function and mitochondrial translation.

PUS1 is expressed in brain and modifies mitochondrial and cytoplasmic tRNAs; MLASA-associated neurodevelopmental involvement is modeled as an incompletely resolved downstream effect of impaired tRNA modification.
Microcephaly is modeled as a neurodevelopmental manifestation of PUS1-related tRNA modification deficiency.
Distichiasis is connected as part of the PUS1-associated developmental phenotype cluster recorded for MLASA.
High palate is connected as part of the PUS1-associated craniofacial phenotype cluster recorded for MLASA.
Long philtrum is connected as part of the PUS1-associated craniofacial phenotype cluster recorded for MLASA.
Micrognathia is connected as part of the PUS1-associated craniofacial phenotype cluster recorded for MLASA.
Short nose is connected as part of the craniofacial phenotype cluster recorded for MLASA.
Defective YARS2 aminoacylation lowers mitochondrial protein synthesis.
Complex V respiratory dysfunction reduces oxidative phosphorylation capacity.
Reduced mitochondrial translation decreases respiratory-chain subunits.
Skeletal muscle oxidative phosphorylation failure causes mitochondrial myopathy.
Skeletal muscle respiratory-chain dysfunction produces the generalized myopathy reported in YARS2-related MLASA.
Impaired mitochondrial ATP production in skeletal muscle limits exertional energy capacity.
Chronic mitochondrial myopathy is modeled as the upstream context for generalized limb muscle atrophy.
Hypotonia is modeled as a neuromuscular manifestation of mitochondrial myopathy and energy failure.
Respiratory-chain dysfunction shifts metabolism toward lactate production.
The broad metabolism/homeostasis abnormality is grounded in impaired oxidative phosphorylation and lactic acidosis.
Respiratory-chain dysfunction shifts pyruvate handling toward lactate accumulation.
Disturbed lactate metabolism in MLASA can include elevated pyruvate on organic-acid analysis.
Respiratory-chain enzyme deficiency is a biochemical tissue readout of the same mitochondrial dysfunction node.
Myopathic muscle involvement can produce abnormal electromyography.
Bone marrow mitochondrial dysfunction disrupts erythroid maturation and iron handling.
Cardiac energy stress can produce hypertrophic cardiomyopathy in YARS2-related disease.
Respiratory muscle weakness can lead to respiratory insufficiency.
Delayed puberty is connected as a multisystem manifestation recorded in the MLASA Orphanet phenotype profile; the intermediate mechanism is unresolved.
Glaucoma is connected as an ophthalmologic manifestation recorded in the MLASA Orphanet phenotype profile; the intermediate mechanism is unresolved.
Chronic myopathy and axial weakness are modeled as contributing context for scoliosis in MLASA.
Chronic myopathy and axial weakness are modeled as contributing context for kyphosis in MLASA.
Elevated lactate reports impaired oxidative phosphorylation with compensatory lactate production.
Elevated lactate is the biochemical basis of the lactic acidosis phenotype.
Elevated pyruvate with lactate reflects disturbed mitochondrial energy metabolism.
Lower respiratory-chain complex activity reports greater mitochondrial respiratory-chain dysfunction.
Reduced Pus1p-dependent tRNA pseudouridylation is the biochemical readout of PUS1 loss of function.
Lower catalytic efficiency reports impaired YARS2 mitochondrial tyrosyl-tRNA aminoacylation.

nodes.description: Orphanet records this broad metabolism/homeostasis HPO annotation; in MLASA, the specific evidence-backed metabolic manifestation is lactic acidosis.

nodes.description: Impaired mitochondrial translation or complex V function reduces oxidative phosphorylation in skeletal muscle and other high-energy tissues and disrupts erythroid maturation in bone marrow.

evidence.snippet: Muscle studies showed global cytochrome-c oxidase deficiency in all patients tested and severe, combined respiratory chain complex activity deficiencies.

evidence.reference_title: Missense mutation in pseudouridine synthase 1 (PUS1) causes mitochondrial myopathy and sideroblastic anemia (MLASA).
evidence.snippet: Mitochondrial myopathy and sideroblastic anemia (MLASA) is a rare, autosomal recessive oxidative phosphorylation disorder specific to skeletal muscle and bone marrow.

nodes.description: PUS1 and YARS2 defects converge on impaired mitochondrial translation, reducing synthesis of respiratory-chain subunits.

evidence.reference_title: Nonsense mutation in pseudouridylate synthase 1 (PUS1) in two brothers affected by myopathy, lactic acidosis and sideroblastic anaemia (MLASA).
evidence.snippet: combined defects in mitochondrial respiratory chain complexes in muscle and fibroblast homogenates of both patients, and low levels of mtDNA translation products in fibroblast mitochondria.

evidence.reference_title: Mutation of the mitochondrial tyrosyl-tRNA synthetase gene, YARS2, causes myopathy, lactic acidosis, and sideroblastic anemia--MLASA syndrome.
evidence.snippet: A mitochondrial protein-synthesis assay showed reduced levels of respiratory chain subunits in myotubes generated from patient cell lines.

evidence.reference_title: Missense mutation in pseudouridine synthase 1 (PUS1) causes mitochondrial myopathy and sideroblastic anemia (MLASA).
evidence.snippet: identified a homozygous missense mutation in the pseudouridine synthase 1 gene (PUS1) in all patients with MLASA from these families.

evidence.reference_title: Mitochondrial myopathy and sideroblastic anemia (MLASA): missense mutation in the pseudouridine synthase 1 (PUS1) gene is associated with the loss of tRNA pseudouridylation.
evidence.snippet: MLASA is thus associated with absent or greatly reduced tRNA pseudouridylation at specific sites, implicating this pathway in its molecular pathogenesis.

nodes.description: Biallelic PUS1 variants impair Pus1p-mediated pseudouridylation of mitochondrial and cytoplasmic tRNAs.

nodes.description: No curative treatment is established for MLASA overall. Management is supportive, with early genetic diagnosis enabling monitoring and treatment of cardiomyopathy, respiratory muscle weakness, anemia, nutrition, and other complications.

evidence.reference_title: A distinct mitochondrial myopathy, lactic acidosis and sideroblastic anemia (MLASA) phenotype associates with YARS2 mutations.
evidence.snippet: Although no specific curative therapies are available, early diagnosis and initiation of appropriate supportive therapy is likely to be important in the prevention of the longterm complications of this disorder.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical Features, Molecular Heterogeneity, and Prognostic Implications in YARS2-Related Mitochondrial Myopathy.
evidence.snippet: Regular surveillance and early treatment for cardiomyopathy and respiratory muscle weakness is advocated because early treatment may mitigate the significant morbidity and mortality associated with this genetic disorder.

evidence.reference_title: The phenotypic spectrum of germline YARS2 variants: from isolated sideroblastic anemia to mitochondrial myopathy, lactic acidosis and sideroblastic anemia 2.
evidence.snippet: YARS2 p.(Ser203Ile) and p.(Gly244Ala) demonstrated a 17-fold loss in catalytic efficiency.

readouts.interpretation: Lower catalytic efficiency reports impaired YARS2 mitochondrial tyrosyl-tRNA aminoacylation.

nodes.description: Biallelic YARS2 variants reduce mitochondrial tyrosyl-tRNA synthetase catalytic efficiency and impair aminoacylation needed for mitochondrial protein synthesis.

biological_processes: tRNA aminoacylation for mitochondrial protein translation

evidence.reference_title: The phenotypic spectrum of germline YARS2 variants: from isolated sideroblastic anemia to mitochondrial myopathy, lactic acidosis and sideroblastic anemia 2.
evidence.snippet: YARS2 encodes the mitochondrial tyrosyl-tRNA synthetase, YARS2, which is responsible for the ATP-dependent conjugation of tyrosine to its cognate tRNA, required to support mitochondrial protein synthesis.

evidence.reference_title: The phenotypic spectrum of germline YARS2 variants: from isolated sideroblastic anemia to mitochondrial myopathy, lactic acidosis and sideroblastic anemia 2.
evidence.snippet: YARS2 p.(Ser203Ile) and p.(Gly244Ala) demonstrated a 17-fold loss in catalytic efficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Mutation of the mitochondrial tyrosyl-tRNA synthetase gene, YARS2, causes myopathy, lactic acidosis, and sideroblastic anemia--MLASA syndrome.
evidence.snippet: We propose that the reduced aminoacylation activity of mutant YARS2 enzyme leads to decreased mitochondrial protein synthesis, resulting in mitochondrial respiratory chain dysfunction.

evidence.reference_title: Mutation of the mitochondrial tyrosyl-tRNA synthetase gene, YARS2, causes myopathy, lactic acidosis, and sideroblastic anemia--MLASA syndrome.
evidence.snippet: We subsequently identified the same mutation in another unrelated MLASA patient.

evidence.reference_title: The phenotypic spectrum of germline YARS2 variants: from isolated sideroblastic anemia to mitochondrial myopathy, lactic acidosis and sideroblastic anemia 2.
evidence.snippet: biallelic YARS2 variants, including severe loss-of-function alleles in trans of the common p.(Gly191Val) variant, should be considered as a cause of isolated congenital sideroblastic anemia, as well as the MLASA syndromic phenotype.

evidence.reference_title: Mitochondrial myopathy and sideroblastic anemia (MLASA): missense mutation in the pseudouridine synthase 1 (PUS1) gene is associated with the loss of tRNA pseudouridylation.
evidence.snippet: MLASA is thus associated with absent or greatly reduced tRNA pseudouridylation at specific sites, implicating this pathway in its molecular pathogenesis.

readouts.interpretation: Reduced Pus1p-dependent tRNA pseudouridylation is the biochemical readout of PUS1 loss of function.
